California
Fair POlitical
Practices Comrnission
February 27, L99L
Bruce s. Bue1, Manager
McKinleyvi,lIe Community Service District
P. O. Box 2037
McKinleyviIle, CA 9552l-
Re: Your Request for Advj-ce
our File No. A-91-o4 2
Dear Mr. Buel:
You are seeking advice on behalf of McKinleyville Cornmunity
Services District ('rdistrict'r) directors Joe wal-und and Edward
Estes regarding their duties and responsibilities under the
confl- ictlof- interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
gs1rr1.1 Pursuant to Regulation 18329 (copy enclosed), we provide
yo, ,ith advice as the two directors' authorized representative,
but because no specrfic governmental decision is pending this
advice is informal onlY. z
The foll,owing advice is based upon the facts provided in your
Ietter of January 14 | l99]- and in your prior telephone
conversation with this agencY.
OUESTION
A parcel of land wiII either be purchased through
condemnition by the district 
for a community park, or developed by
its owner and Lherefore require sewer and water services.
Directors walund and Estes own and live in their respective
residences rnore than 3oo feet, but less than 2500 feet, from the
parcel. can they participate in either the condemnation decision
or the decision Lo provide sewer and water services?
1 co.r"..,."nt code Sections 81ooo-91015. A11 statutory references
are to the Governnent code unless otherwise indicated. 
Commission
regulations appqer at 2 California code of ReguLations Section
18ooo, et seq. AfI references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the Catifornia code of Regulations.
2 
Info..al assistance does not provide the requestor wlth the
irnmunity provided by an opinion or formal rrritten advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329 (c) . )
Street, Suite 8OO . P.O. Box 8O7 o Sacramento CA 958O4-O8O7 . (916) 322-5660
428 J


File No. A-91-o42
Page 2
CONCLUS ION
Both directors may participate in the forthcoming district
decj,sions concerning the parcel provided the decisions will not
have a foreseeable naterial financial impact on their respective
properties.
FACTS
The district will be making decisions concerning a 78-acre
parcel of Land. The district witl either purchase the parcel
through condemnation for a community park, or the owner of the
parcel wiII develop the parcel and request the district 
to provide
sewer and water services. District Directors Joe walund and
Ed!,rard Estes both live within 2500 feet, but more than 300 feet,
fron the parcel. 
You have indicated that approxinately 400
individuals live in 150 homes within 25oo feet of the parcel, and
that single family homes in the neighborhood currently se1I for
$l-5o,00o to 920o,000.
ANALYSIS
The Act prohibits a public official 
from making,
participating in, or using his or her official position to
influence a governmental decision in which the official 
knows or
has reason to know he or she has a financial interest. 
(section
87100.) A public official 
has a financial interest in a decision
if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will- have a
material financial effect, distinguishable fron its effect on the
public generally, on the offj,cial, a member of official's
irnrnediate family, or on any real property in which the official-
has a direct or indirect interest worth one thousand ($1,000) or
more. (Section 87103 (b) . )
Directors of the district 
are public officiaLs. 
(Section
A2O4a. ) It is understood that Mr. walund and !lr. Estes each has
an interest in their respective properties that is worth one
thousand dollars or rnore. Therefore, they must disqualify
themselves fron any district 
decision which will have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on them or on their real
property interests that is distinguishabLe from the effect on the
public Aenerally. (Section 87103 (b) . )
Foreseeable uaterial Financial Effect
The effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial, likelihood that it witl occur. While certainty
is not required, an effect that is nerely a possibility is not
reasonabl-y foreseeable. (Do$/nev cares v. Do$rnev comnunitv
Development Com. (1987) l-96 Cal.App.3d 983,' fn re Thorner (1975)
l-TFFE-ojr- 1get'
In order to deterrni,ne whether the foreseeable effect of a
decision is material- as to real- property in which an official 
has
an interest, the Commission has adopted Regulation !8702.3 (copy
enclosed) . 
You have inf orrned us that both Directors Walund and


File No. A-91-O42
Page 3
Estes own property located !'rithin 2,5OO, but more than 300, feet
from the site of the proposed developnent. For projects bett een
3oO and 2,5oo feet from an official's 
property, the effect of the
decision will be considered material if it will have a reasonably
foreseeable financial effect of:
(A) Ten thousand dol]ars (S10,00o) or more on
the fair market value of the real property in which
the official- has an interest; or
(B) Will affect the rental value of the
property by $1,ooo ore tnore per l-2 month period.
Regulation 1a702.3(a) (3) (A) and (B)
whether the effect of the decision is positive or neqative is of
no consequence under the Act. 
(Yound Advice Letter, A-89-149,
copy enclosed. )
The thro directors' participation in decisions on the parcel
is permitted if the impact of the district's 
decisions on their
respective properties faIls below the financial thresholds
spelifiea in the regulation. 
whether the irnpact does or does not
i! a factual determination. The Commission does not act as a
finder of fact.3 
However, $re do wish to drar., your attention to
Regutation 18702.3(d). This regulation provides some_ factors to
coisider in determining the rnagnitude of the financial impact on
both directors' properties.{ 
we note that none of the factors
provided in this regulation are addressed in your letter.
The .rPublic Generallyrr ExcePtion
If you conclude that the requisite financial i.mpact as
provided for in Regulation La7o2.3 (a) (3) is not present,. it is
i:nnec"=sary to determine whether the impact on the two directors
is distinguishable from the effect on the public Aenerally. If
the financial irnpact is present, Mr. Walund and Mr. Estes may not
participate in decisions concerning the parcel if the effect of
the decision on their interests is distinguishable from the effect
on the public generally. (section 87103.)
3 B""ao=" the commission does not act as a finder of fact, any
advice provided would be appl,icable only to the extent that facts
have been provided, are correct, and that all material facts have
been presented. (In re Oqlesbv, (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71, copy
enc losed . )
4 such factors include the proximity of the properties and the
magnitude of the^ parcel- project in relationship to the directors'
pr6perties, wnet'hlr it ii r6asonably foreseeable that the
decisions on the parcel wilt affect the developnent potential or
income producing potential of the directors' properties, and
r"/hether it is reasonably foreseeable that the parcel decisions
rril} result in a change of the character of the neighborhood
including, but not Iimited to, the effect on traffic, 
view,
privacy, intens j-ty of use, noise leveIs, air enissions or similar
tra its .


FlIe No, A-97-O42
Page 4
The financial effect of the district's 
decisi'on on the
directors is distinguishable fron the effect on the public
generally unfess the decision lnlill affect their properties in
iubstantially the same manner as j,t will' affect al'1 members of the
public or a significant seg:ment of the public. (Regulation l'8703;
]oroensen Advice Letter, No. A-9o-017; In re l.,egan (1985) 9 FPPC
ops. f; In re owen (1975) 2 FPPC OPs- 77, copies enclosed.)
The t'public generallyrr is comprised of the entire
jurisdiction of the agency in question. (In re Leqanl s99fa')
i.egulation 18703 also permits €he application of the "public
geierally" exception $rhen a decision affects the official's
interest! in substantially the same manner as it wiII affect a
significant segrnent of the public. 
Hor"/ever, you have.not provided
anf facts to indicate how the financial effect of decisions
coircerning the parcel will affect either atl district 
residents or
a signifi-ant 
segment of the district's 
residents. For that
reas6n, we are unable to provide you with advice on whether the
;pubtic generally" exception applies in this situation.5
I hope this tetter has provided you with suf f j-cient general
guidance. If you have any further guestions reqarding this
matter, please contact ne at (916) 322-59or.
Sincerely,
Scott
Act j.ng
Ha I labr in
cenera 1
S. Rothman
LegaI Division
sH : JSR: ken
Enc l osure s
5 Th" factors discussed previously in Regulation 18702.3(d) would
be relevant in any consideration of whether the pubti-c qeneralLy
exception wouldopply. Al-so, a group that is large in nunber and
heteiogeneous in quatity can constitute a significant segment of
ttre pubtic for the purposes of the public generally exception.
(In ie Ferraro (1978) 4 FPPC Ops. 62.) To assist you in
eva:.uating the ttpublic generallyrr exception, I have enclosed
copi,es of other Cornrnission advice letters which have addressed the
isiue. 
Enclosed are: west Advice Letter, No. A-89-243; Scher
Advice Letter, No. A-88-479; Flvnn Advice Letter, No. f-88-430.
